title: Deep Strong light-matter Coupling in 3D Kane Fermions
authors: Dmitriy Yavorskiy, David Hagenmuller, Noureddine Charrouj, Yurii Ivonyak, Alexander Kazakov, Yanko Todorov, Wojciech Knap, Marcin Bialek
abstract: Deep strong light-matter coupling represents an extreme non-perturbative regime of quantum electrodynamics, in which the interaction strength exceeds the bare frequencies of the uncoupled systems. The ground state features strong quantum correlations between photons and matter excitations, and new cavity-driven phase transitions are expected to occur. Whether a superradiant quantum phase transition, marked by spontaneous dipole ordering and photon condensation, is possible has remained a long-standing and controversial question. Such phenomena have been proposed to arise in exotic electronic systems hosting Dirac and Kane fermions, owing to the formal absence of an $A^2$ term in their low-energy Hamiltonian. Here we exploit the ultralow effective mass of Kane fermions to realise Landau polaritons in a bulk mercury cadmium telluride layer coupled to a Fabry-Perot resonator. Using thermally tunable carrier density, we continuously tune the coupling from the weak to the deep-strong regime, achieving a record normalised coupling ratio exceeding 1.6 above room temperature. The measured polariton spectra are in excellent agreement with a rigorous, gauge-invariant microscopic theory. Despite the nonlinear Landau level structure of relativistic Kane fermions, we show that a diamagnetic $A^2$ term naturally emerges and precludes a superradiant phase transition. These results resolve the long-standing controversy surrounding cavity quantum electrodynamics of relativistic-like matter systems, extend deep-strong-coupling physics to Kane fermions, and open new opportunities for polaritonic semiconductor devices operating in extreme light-matter coupling regimes.
summary: The study achieves record-breaking deep strong light-matter coupling in 3D Kane fermions at room temperature. It resolves a long-standing debate by showing that a diamagnetic term naturally emerges to prevent a superradiant phase transition. This work opens new avenues for developing polaritonic semiconductor devices in extreme coupling regimes.
main_problem: Resolving the controversy regarding whether a superradiant quantum phase transition can occur in relativistic-like electronic systems (Dirac/Kane fermions) due to the potential absence of the diamagnetic A^2 term.
main_result: Demonstrated the deep strong-coupling regime with a record coupling ratio exceeding 1.6 at room temperature and proved that a gauge-invariant A^2 term emerges, precluding the superradiant phase transition.
method: THz time-domain spectroscopy in reflection mode combined with a gauge-invariant microscopic quantum model and transfer-matrix simulations.
model_or_system: 3D Kane fermions in a bulk Mercury Cadmium Telluride (MCT) layer coupled to a Fabry-Perot resonator, modeled using a Hopfield-like Hamiltonian derived via a projected unitary gauge transformation.
key_observables: Reflectivity spectra, electric field spectra, cyclotron resonance transitions, and polariton dispersion.
important_parameters: Normalized coupling ratio (eta > 1.6), temperature-dependent carrier density, magnetic field, and the ultralow effective mass of Kane fermions.
main_assumptions: Low-excitation regime where electronic transitions are mapped to bosonic operators, and neglect of spin-flip excitations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the temperature evolution of the MCT band structure and carrier density; Figure 2 illustrates the cavity design, electric field distribution, and temperature-dependent cavity modes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the deep strong-coupling problem, describes the MCT/cavity experimental platform, presents THz spectroscopy results, details the microscopic gauge-invariant theoretical model, and concludes by resolving the superradiant phase transition controversy.
why_interesting: It addresses a fundamental debate in cavity QED regarding gauge invariance and the existence of phase transitions in light-matter systems with relativistic-like matter, while demonstrating extreme non-perturbative coupling regimes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 Kane fermions in MCT. (a) Temperature evolution of the electronic band structure from an inverted semimetallic phase (EG < 0) at low temperatures, to a normal semiconducting phase (EG > 0) at high temperatures. At the critical temperature Tc ≈100 K (for x = 0.15), the band structure becomes gapless. At this point, the bands exhibit linear momentum dispersion and host massless Kane fermions. With increasing temperature, the Fermi level rises into the conduction band (red). (b) Top, temperature dependence of the electron density in MCT; coloured stars mark the tem- peratures corresponding to the reflection spectra shown in Fig. 2. Bottom, temperature dependence of the Fermi energy (EF...

Fig. 2 Sample and cavity modes. (a) Heterostructure schematic indicates the active MCT layer as the red block, which is sandwiched by ∼1 µm-thick MCT layers with Cd composition continuously graded from 45% to 15% (grey blocks). We probe the sample using THz-TDS in reflection mode. (b) Temperature evolution of M1 and M2 modes in reflection spectra (solid lines). M0 1 and M0 2 denote the modes in the absence of carriers in the MCT layer (dashed lines). (c) Calculated real part of the electric field profile in the cavity at zero magnetic field and selected temperatures (d) Reflection spectra at the same selected temperatures. Arrows mark M1 and M2 modes.

Fig. 3 LL dispersion and cyclotron transitions in MCT. (a) Energy dispersion of the conduction-band LL En,kz,σ at B = 0.45 T and T = 30 K (electron density ρ = 1.48 × 1015 cm−3). The Fermi energy EF is shown as a horizontal black line. Filled states lie within the shaded area. Blue (red) curves denote the σ = −1 (σ = +1) sector. Dipole-allowed, σ-conserving transitions between the highest occupied and lowest empty LLs are marked by arrows. As kz moves from left to right across the vertical blue (red) dotted line, the filling factors ν−(ν+) decrease from 2 (1) to 1 (0). (b) Cyclotron gap ∆kz,σ between the last filled and first empty LLs as a function of magnetic field B, forming...

Fig. 4 Reflection spectra. (a) Reflection as a function of magnetic field at selected temperatures. Dotted lines indicate the CR. At 10 K, two horizontal dashed lines mark photonic modes M0 1 and M0 2. Continuous lines are eigenvalues of Eq. (2), which are labelled in the 50 K segment—the upper and lower co-rotating polaritons as crUP and crLP, respectively, and the anti-rotating polariton as aRP. (b) Simulated spectra based on the transfer-matrix model. The lines are identical to those in panel (a).

Fig. 5 Microscopic modelling of MCT polaritons. (a) Schematic representation of the Hamil- tonian (2). The CR collective “bright” mode arises as a coherent superposition of transitions from the highest occupied LL to the lowest unoccupied level, contributed by many transverse wavevectors kz. This mode couples resonantly to the co-rotating photon modes M0 j (j = 1, 2) with strength Ωj,

Fig. 6 Deep strong coupling figures of merit for MCT Landau polaritons. (a) Temperature dependence of the normalized coupling strength Ωj/ωj for the photon modes M0 j (j = 1, 2). (b) Ground-state photon populations in the first cavity mode M0 1, for left- (pink) and right- (blue) circular polarisations, ⟨a† 1,pa1,p⟩(p = l, r), together with the inter-polarization correlations ⟨a† 1,la† 1,r⟩.
